speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Arnold, you were discussing the Russian invasion and that there was a Hitler-Stalin pact. Could you elaborate more on the changes in your school and what you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. The school became a Russian-speaking school. Most of the children were still pretty much the same children. It happened to be the closest school to our house, perhaps half a mile walk. And there were no school buses in those days. So uh, in the winter, uh, when school day started, it was still dark. And usually, we had a foot of snow to walk through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I guess my parents felt that might as well stay in the school that was close by. So there was really no reason transferring. Uh, the main difference was there was a Russian-- main, main, uh, teaching language was Russian. So that's how I learned Russian. Very short time, I became fluent in Russian. And the date? It was 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1939 and 19, 1940 and 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were eight, nine-years-old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So uh, a, the, the, the, the teaching language was Russian. But, uh, they did recognize the different ethnic groups. And they knew who was Jewish. That was part of your record. So they were teaching, uh, Yiddish to all the Jewish children. That's how I learned how to read and write in Yiddish. It turns out to be easier than reading and writing Hebrew, because you don't have to worry about vowels. Uh, at the same time, Ukrainians, for example, had a separate class that Ukrainians are. So I never learned much Ukrainian. But, uh, I did learn a little bit of Yiddish in those classes. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: They, they separated the language classes or the, all the classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They separated the children according to their ethnic origin so that the Jews would be studying Yiddish and not Hebrew, Yiddish. Hebrew was forbidden, illegal, didn't exist. But, but, uh, Yiddish was official.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, Jewish language.

speaker: interviewer
text: One class a day that you were separated or all classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember if it was a daily class. But it was, uh, perhaps twice a week or once a week. So I, I remember that, that much. Uh, the, the school was quite advanced. Even in second grade, we, uh, had mathematical problems that were quite difficult. I remember. Uh, had a bit of problem trying to solve these, uh, mathematical, uh, uh, puzzles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father help me. He was pretty good at it. But the interesting thing is that many of these problems, when I brought them home, my father had to use algebra to solve these. Of course, I didn't know algebra at that time. So they expected the children to actually be able to reason out the solution, which normally one would solve by algebra with one or two unknowns. But uh, so th-that was the, the kind of, uh, level, uh, that mathematics was taught at in Russian schools. really good. There were several Russian children in the class, too. They were children of the Russian officials who came, uh, uh, to, to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father was the physician for the [INAUDIBLE], for the secret police, which brought certain privileges, uh, there. He would attend to the families of their high officials. Uh, one of the privileges that came with it is that he had a car available, not for his personal use, but car was always available for him to go on, uh, on house calls. He had a chauffeur, a driver.

speaker: interviewee
text: And uh, sometimes he would take me along. I remember once, I went along, uh, on a trip. It, it was a gray Buick. I remember that it was a very fancy car. I don't know how the Russians got a Buick. There were not very many those, uh, in Poland. Must have been con-confiscated from some rich Pole, uh, when the Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And h-how, what were some other changes affecting your mother's life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me finish this particular story. Uh, the, uh, that's one of the things I remember. Um, we went on a trip. Um, my father, uh, went to see the patient. And, uh, the chauffeur said to, to me, I'm going out for a minute. Why don't you stay in the car? Just make sure you don't touch anything, especially don't touch, uh, the gear stick, because the car will start moving.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then he left. And I thought, well, he's just trying to, uh, be smart with me, because I knew, you know, you have to use the brake to start, stop the car or the engine. And what's the gear stick got to do with having the car moving? So I started fooling around with the gear stick. I pulled it from reverse to neutral. And the car started rolling down the hill with me in the car. Fortunately, the car stopped soon against the curb. So, uh, he didn't say anything to father. I guess he wasn't supposed to leave the car and me alone in the car. So, uh, that, that particular incident, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as my mother, we still had a maid. It was not the same maid, uh, that we had before the war. I don't quite un-- remember the circumstances why the other two maids left. But we did have a maid throughout the, uh, uh, Russian occupation period. So we lived pretty comfortably still. My mother still had social life. And uh, there were, I remember, some, several Russian high officers would come to, uh, to stay with us temporarily, I guess, when they were becoming re-relocated. Uh, they had to have a temporary place. There are not very many hotels, so. Uh, one of them was, uh very friendly, brought me a BB gun, which I was very fond of. So, uh, I guess those were pretty good years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the new maid that you had, was she Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was not Russian. I, I guess she was either Polish or Ukrainian. She had, uh, I remember she had a number of Russian, uh, soldier boyfriends. Sometimes, they would come to visit. One of them was a, uh, was a pilot in the Russian air force. And I was hoping that maybe she could persuade him to take me for a flight in a, in a fighter plane, but [LAUGHS] never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your life basically happy at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say so, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, uh, had no, uh, no particular problems or concerns. Uh, it was pretty good. Uh, nobody really knew what was going to happen, how long the peace with, uh, with Russia and Germany would last, because the war was still going on in the west. So, but I wasn't concerned with these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the dates when, of the German-Stalin pact and the changes in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, uh, I think it was June, 1941. And it looked like history repeated itself. Nobody knew anything was coming. But all of a sudden, again, there were German planes overhead dropping bombs. So that was the first sign. And, uh, we went to the bomb shelter again. And didn't take very long for the Germans to, uh, to, uh, to, to come, because they didn't have very far to go. Uh, so, uh, the first day after the war started, number of Russians started fleeing to Russia. Several of them stayed. I remember there were several Russians. I don't know why they stayed. Um, one of my father's patients-- there are certain things that I do remember-- one of my father's patients was a Russian official. He happened to be a drug addict. And my father supplied him with, with morphine. And I remember he didn't go back with the Russians. He stayed. And even after Germans came, my father would go and see him and give him morphine injections. So, uh, that was a very scary time, perhaps not for me. I didn't quite understand very well what was happening. But one day, I remember we were sitting in the, uh, in the bomb shelter. And, uh, Germans came. The door opened. And, uh, I heard German voices [INAUDIBLE], saying, [GERMAN]. All the Jews out. And he waited a few minutes. Nobody got out, even though most people there were Jewish. And he left. So that was my first encounter with the, uh, with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1930--

speaker: interviewee
text: J-June, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice if any other Jewish families in your town left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, well, in the last days, I, I, I, I can't think of anybody who, who left with the Russians. There were some people who were lucky enough to be exiled by the Russians into Siberia, which was a terrible thing in those days. They would pretty much arbitrarily choose certain people who thought were, uh, subversive. And they would, uh, exile them to Siberia. And, uh, in fact, most of the Jews whom I met after the war who survived, uh, were in that category. They, they, they escaped because they were sent away by the Russians. Uh, but when the war started, I guess there wasn't very much time to run. And there was no place to run, so I, I can't think of anybody who, uh, escaped that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So Germans came. We still were in our apartment, temporarily. Uh, we had several encounters with the Germans that I remember. Um, once two German soldiers came in. I guess they would just arbitrarily go, uh, go, uh, to houses and look around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, my mother invited them in-- I remember that-- and gave them some tea. And, uh, the other one said to them-- I, I didn't understand German in those days, but mother told me later what they were saying-- the other soldier said to the first one, are these Jews? So the first soldier said, oh, no, this is a Polish lady. And I guess my mother didn't say anything. So they were very pleasant. And, uh, uh, one of them sat down at the piano and played, played, my music, Clementi sonatinas. I remember. He was trying to show off, played it much too fast. But, uh, he was pretty good, I thought. So that was one encounter I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then an officer, uh, a German officer, came in to look around the apartment. We had some fancy antique furniture in the dining room and the living room and many paintings. I remember several paintings. One was where the, uh, man is with a sword, ready to cut the baby and mother begging the king to, to stop it. So that was one painting I remember. Another one was, uh, a priest and a rabbi arguing over, uh, the Bible. I don't-- I'm not sure who, who the artists were. But, uh, I do remember the oil paintings, several paintings like this. Another painting that was in my room was of a girl skating on a thin ice and a guardian angel behind her. I remember that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he would just point at the different paintings. I want this. I want this. I want this, half of the furniture, the dining room table, and so on, so. The German soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an officer-- captain or colonel, whatever. Uh, my father was arguing with him at some point. I guess he didn't know what, when to stop. So the officer hit him and knocked a couple of teeth out. But, uh, fortunately, that's all he did. And a few days later, the furniture was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in that apartment for, uh, several months, uh, perhaps, two or three months. Then the Germans ordered, uh, all the Jews to move out. They were starting to form the ghetto. So the part of the, uh, the part of the, uh, sector in town was cordoned off for the Jews. So I remember, we took whatever we could, uh, and moved in. It actually was pretty large apartment by the river. It was rather comfortable. [INAUDIBLE] the apartment-- Where you were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were not sent. We had, we had to leave our apartment. And somehow, my parents located a place to stay, happened to be an empty apartment. So we stayed there. We took some of our belongings with us, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a few small pieces of furniture that were left, bedding, clothes, that kind of thing, nothing really much, my father's medical, uh, stuff, medi-medicines, and, uh, some of the instruments. Uh, the house was very nicely located. I remember it didn't have an indoor bathroom. But o-other than that, it was a pretty good apartment. It was located right next to Bishop's Castle, uh, Estate. So the, that estate had beautiful garden. It was fenced off. And I liked to go into, uh, that garden. There was a hole in the fence. And I liked to spend time in that garden. It was very quiet and green.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a priest caught me there once and chased me out. And I was thinking, I mean, he's a priest. They are supposed to be holy people. Why, why, why are they, so, so mean? So we stayed in that house, uh, it was near the river. I remember going swimming. It was late summer. And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1941, I think. Um, then what happened was that the, uh, number of Jewish doctors managed to find a house, which was a very nice estate house with a large garden. And that became a medical, uh, residence for these doctors. So we moved there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So each family had one room. So in our room, there was, uh, my father, mother, myself, my sister, our piano teacher, and our cousin Gisella, who stayed with us. Uh, at that time, she, uh, managed to get the Christian papers. So as I remember, uh, she moved out and lived with a Christian family. So that's how she s-survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there was a common kitchen, common bathroom, and a very nice garden. So, uh, we did very well. We were never hungry. Uh, my father still had patients. Non-Jews could come into the ghetto. Jews were not allowed to go out. But he had a number of, uh, farmers from the outskirts. And money, of course, was not worth very much in those days. So they would bring in chickens and, and, and, meat and eggs and milk and bread. So we had enough food because of that. And, uh, I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he give the farmers in return?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the medical service. They came to see the doctor. And he would see them. Uh, I don't remember exactly where he saw them. There must have been a spare room for, uh, as a medical office.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was--

speaker: interviewee
text: But sometimes, he would also go see them in their houses and then bring the produce with him. As a doctor, he would, he was allowed to, uh, to go out with a, with a certain permit. Everybody had to wear a, uh, a yellow patch-- children, babies in carriages. Everybody had to wear a yellow patch. A doctor had, in addition to the patch, a band with a red star of David and, uh, [? arts ?] physician, uh, written on it. So I guess had special privileges because of that. Uh, I, I remember there was a lot of hunger around, because children would come in. And my mother would cook large pots of soup and other food. And children would come in and, and have a good meal there. Uh, but uh, as time went on, there was more and more starvation, because there is no way to get food. People were given rations but, uh, not very much-- piece of bread for a week or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz Ghetto, I guess. I don't know there was any particular name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a wall or barrier around the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember seeing any wall. There must have been some kind of a barricade through which one had to go to, to, to get out. Uh, there was, there was no wall that was built, uh, around there. There, there were, uh, Ukrainian police outside, making sure that nobody got out. As I said, people could come in, non-Jews. And then to get out, they had to demonstrate they were not Jewish. Uh, Jews could come out if they had a, uh, special permit to go out. My father could go out. Uh, I suppose you could sneak out. We did eventually sneak out like, like this. It was possible to sneak out, because there was no wall, in the dark especially. There were also Jewish police there. Uh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jewish police treat you or others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Us personally, uh, they, uh, they, they, uh, they tried to be accommodating, I, I imagine. They had to do their job. They had to satisfy the Germans that were keeping order. That was their main function, just to make sure that people behaved themselves, not, not, not, to beat people up or to harass them [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any acts of atrocity by the Ukrainian guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recall of any, no. I, I, I remember, uh, hearing stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there German guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the ghetto? I don't remember seeing any, no. But, uh, as a child and other children, you know, went around living. We played out around in the garden, had a very large garden. Played there in the, in the winter. I remember I had skis and skates. And we went skiing down the hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, we just tried to live as normally as possible. And uh, I didn't go to school. But, uh, my mother tried to, uh, teach me as much as possible. We had some books. So, uh, I did study, uh, a little bit, uh, with my mother. Did your mother ever leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once when we escaped-- we left. I don't remember her leaving, probably not, on any other occasion. What happened when, uh, ghetto was liquidated-- I guess it must have been August of '40, '41, uh, our chauffeur, he was a Ukrainian. Uh, he, uh, had some German friends. And, uh, I guess they were sitting and drinking, uh, one evening. And they told him that the following day, the next day, there is going to be a big action, which means liquidation of the ghetto. And everybody was going to get shot. So he came, uh, and warned, uh, my father that this was going to happen. So, uh, when it got dark, we, we walked out. Before this, before this incident, we tried to get out. And, and, uh, we knew that this was come sooner or later, that, uh, it was no secret that the ghetto was going to be liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, uh, my parents tried to find a place to, uh, to hide. For some time before, I think that was before even before we moved into this medical residence building, we spent some times with some patients of my father's outside of town. Uh. Polish patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I'm not sure were they Polish, or Czech, or Ukrainian. Perhaps they were Czech. So we stayed in their house. The son of these people was very sick. He was just recently married, had a small baby. And he was very sick. He was not expected to survive. I, I don't know whether he had cancer or whatever. But I guess they felt they let us stay, because that way, father could be next, close to him all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after several days or a week, uh, the man died. There was a big wake. And I still remember, uh, his father, uh, was making a speech. And he says, well, the doctor, even though he was a Jew, he did the best he could. And so we're not blaming him for anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after this, I guess my father felt that it was not a good idea to stay there. So we went back. And we moved into this medical, uh, house. Well, my father had some other patients who, uh, I'm not sure whether he actually discussed it with them before or not, but when it got dark on a day before liquidation, uh, we took just a few belongings, including my father's diploma, and we walked over. Nobody saw us. We walked over to, uh, to that place. This was a Czech farmer family. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. I remember that. I don't remember his wife's name. And they had two children, a daughter about 17-- her name was Ella-- and a son about 19.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, uh, they said, well-- I guess they felt obligated to, to, to my father, because he, uh, he treated them number of occasions. So, uh, they, they said, well, maybe I can, we can temporarily keep one of you. We definitely can not keep all of you here. So we tried to decide what to do. That still on the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, well, we stayed there overnight. And the following day, ghetto was liquidated. All the Jews were taken out. And most of them were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hear or see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were far away?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew happened. We were far enough away. I didn't, I don't remember hearing any shots. But we definitely knew what happened. And everybody else knew what happened. So there was nobody, nobody was left in the ghetto. Some people managed to run away, uh, not very many. And many of them were caught subsequently.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, anyway, so, so it was decided that my mother would stay there temporarily for a day or two, and my father would go somewhere else to try to find a place for himself to stay. They didn't want to keep me and my sister. So it was decided, we had some, uh, Catholic friends on the other side of town. So my mother thought, well, why don't I and my sister go there? And perhaps they will keep us. So mother stayed behind. My sister and I went, uh, to the other side of town. Uh, we walked through the meadow, which was on the outskirts of town, because hopefully there would be fewer people. Now, anybody who saw a Jew, I guess, was expected to report. Because Jews were not supposed to be walking around on, on the street. So, I, I think we walked on, on the meadow and, uh, holding hands. And I remember the people were looking at this. I guess we were fairly well-known, so people recognized us. They were pointing, looking at us, and whispering, and pointing. And, uh, nobo-nobody said anything. Did you remove your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] Walk with our, uh, yellow patches. Uh, this, this was probably the scariest day of my life, walking like this. And I know my sister was very, very, uh, terrified. Well, we finally got to, uh, this friend's house. She was very upset when she saw us. She said, you mustn't stay here. Just go right back where you came from. We, we can't [? hold you. ?] We can't keep you. So I think she, she gave us some food and waited until it got dark so it would be safer to walk. And, uh, we walked all the way back. That time, it was in the dark, so nobody saw us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were nine years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. So, uh, uh, when that woman, uh, saw us, I guess we must have looked terrible. She, she, she, uh, took pity on us and said, okay, you can stay. So my mother, my sister, and I stayed there that night. They put us up in the barn on top of a, uh, large pile of hay. And we slept there. My father had left already the previous day. And we never saw him again. So we didn't know what was happening. We were hoping that perhaps he found a place for himself. So the next day, uh, the host, uh, came. And he said, all right, I know what I'm going to do with you. I'm going to hide you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had an idea how to do it. There was a large hay stack. It was actually a heap of straw that was leaning against the barn wall. So what he said he would do, would cut out an opening in the wall of the barn, make a door, and then he carved a room inside the, the heap of straw. So we would be sitting there inside that straw stack. The door would open only from the inside, so when, uh, when it was pulled back with some straw against the wall, you couldn't see that there really was, there was inside. So it looked quite inconspicuous from there. So he carved this room. It was wide enough for the three of us to lie down. It was some straw, straw mattress on the floor and cover us with a, uh, feather quilt. And it was tall enough for us to sit down. I guess we're not very tall. So, uh, we, we were able to sit and, uh, and lie down. And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [INAUDIBLE]. So, uh we spent the next year and a half inside this, uh, haystack. The routine was that we slept during the day, and we came out at night. They would bring us food. We went out to the bathroom and washed up and walked a little bit at night. And during the day, we stayed inside. We couldn't see any daylight for a year and a half, practically. Entire time, we didn't see any daylight at all. We had to, uh, sit there. Uh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it? That's half an hour already?
